ut1-1-1.title: Hands-on: Your first pulse-echo measurement
ut1-1-1.intro: Firestone's 1940 pulse-echo idea, live: slide a 0° probe across a stepped steel block and watch the backwall echo arrive later wherever the metal is thicker. Measure three steps and pick out the thinnest — the same single-sided thickness survey run on pipes and vessels every day.

tasks.label: Record thickness readings on three different steps
tasks.hint: Park the probe fully on a step; the backwall echo's round-trip time × velocity ÷ 2 is the thickness.

tasks.label: Identify the thinnest step of the block
tasks.hint: Thinner metal means a shorter round trip — its backwall echo arrives earliest on the A-scan.

ut1-1-2.title: Hands-on: Make wavelength obey λ = v ÷ f
ut1-1-2.intro: Put the master equation to work: sweep the probe frequency and swap between steel, aluminium, and water while the wavefront spacing tracks λ = v ÷ f live. Confirm the lesson's shortcut — λ in mm equals velocity in mm/µs divided by frequency in MHz.

tasks.label: Get the wavelength below 1 mm in steel
tasks.hint: In steel λ = 5.92 ÷ f (mm, with f in MHz) — push the frequency past 5.92 MHz.

tasks.label: Send the pulse through steel, aluminium, and water
tasks.hint: Frequency belongs to the probe and stays put; velocity belongs to the material, so λ jumps at each swap.

ut1-2-1.title: Hands-on: ride a pulse through three materials
ut1-2-1.intro: Fire a probe pulse and watch the crest packet — alternating zones of particle squeeze and stretch — travel the slab. Switch materials to prove velocity belongs to the medium, not the probe.

tasks.label: Send the pulse through steel, aluminium, and water
tasks.hint: Use the material buttons and watch the crest speed change — the probe never changed, the medium did.

tasks.label: Pack the crests under 1 mm apart in steel
tasks.hint: Raise the frequency — each crest is a compression zone, and they sit exactly λ = v/f apart.

ut1-2-2.title: Hands-on: drive λ = v ÷ f with the frequency slider
ut1-2-2.intro: Work the slider lab from this lesson: tune frequency and swap materials while the λ readout tracks v ÷ f. Every move you make is the field trade between sensitivity and penetration.

tasks.label: Shrink λ below 1 mm in steel for small-flaw sensitivity
tasks.hint: Field shortcut: λ(mm) ≈ 5.92 ÷ f(MHz) in steel, so you need roughly 6 MHz or more.

tasks.label: Hold f fixed and compare λ in steel, aluminium, water
tasks.hint: Frequency belongs to the probe and never changes — only v changes, so λ stretches or shrinks with it.

ut1-2-3.title: Hands-on: harvest echoes from the steel–air mirror
ut1-2-3.intro: Slide a 0° probe across a stepped block: every back-wall echo exists because the steel–air interface reflects essentially 100% of the sound. Read each echo's round-trip time as metal path via d = v × t ÷ 2.

tasks.label: Record the back-wall echo on three different steps
tasks.hint: These steps double each time — watch the echo's round-trip time double with the thickness.

tasks.label: Identify the step with the earliest echo
tasks.hint: Least metal means the shortest round trip: pick the step whose echo sits closest to the initial pulse.

ut1-2-4.title: Hands-on: hunt holes with a 60° refracted shear beam
ut1-2-4.intro: Slide the wedge along the welded plate — its incident angle sits between the two critical angles, so Snell's law puts a single 60° shear wave into the steel. Mark each hidden side-drilled hole where its echo peaks.

tasks.label: Mark the first hole at its echo peak
tasks.hint: Watch the A-scan while you slide: press Mark within ±4 mm of the position where the echo stands tallest.

tasks.label: Mark the second hole — deeper means a longer sound path
tasks.hint: A deeper hole returns its echo later along the first-leg path; keep sliding until a fresh peak rises.

tasks.label: Mark the third hole to clear the whole weld
tasks.hint: A hole only answers when the refracted beam centreline actually crosses it — sweep the full scan line.

ut1-3-1.title: Hands-on: Read the live A-scan on a stepped block
ut1-3-1.intro: Drag the 0-degree probe across the stepped block and watch the back-wall echo slide along the time base — horizontal position is time of flight, vertical height is returned energy. Log readings on three steps, then use the sweep alone to call the thinnest one.

tasks.label: Record A-scan readings on three different steps
tasks.hint: Center the probe on a step; the back-wall echo lands farther right on the sweep as the steel under it gets thicker.

tasks.label: Pick the step whose back-wall echo arrives earliest
tasks.hint: Least metal means shortest time of flight — choose the step whose echo sits closest to the initial pulse.

ut1-3-2.title: Hands-on: Peak the SDH echoes that set sensitivity
ut1-3-2.intro: A reference reflector only means something at its echo peak. Slide the angle-beam wedge over two side-drilled holes — the same known reflectors a procedure names for setting the reference level — and mark each one exactly where its A-scan echo maximizes.

tasks.label: Maximize and mark the first side-drilled hole
tasks.hint: Inch the wedge until the echo stops growing, then mark within 4 mm of peak — the same move as maximizing a cal reflector.

tasks.label: Maximize and mark the second side-drilled hole
tasks.hint: Keep sliding past the first hole; a fresh echo rises as the beam centreline approaches the next SDH.

ut1-3-3.title: Hands-on: Gauge a thin step wedge with T = vt/2
ut1-3-3.intro: A digital gauge just times the round trip and computes thickness as velocity times time over two. Work the probe across a thin-wall step wedge, record three readings, and flag the minimum wall — the number a corrosion survey exists to find.

tasks.label: Take gauge readings on three different steps
tasks.hint: Hold the probe flat on each step — the readout follows T = vt/2 from the timed back-wall echo.

tasks.label: Flag the thinnest step as the critical wall
tasks.hint: Sweep all four steps and compare numbers — the minimum wall is what the survey reports.

ut1-4-1.title: Hands-on: dial the element, frequency sets wavelength
ut1-4-1.intro: A transducer's frequency is built in at manufacture: the element is ground to half a wavelength thick. Raise the drive frequency (a thinner crystal) and watch the wavelength it launches shrink, then switch media to see that f stays the element's property while lambda belongs to the material.

tasks.label: Pick a thinner element: push lambda in steel below 1 mm
tasks.hint: Lambda = v/f. In steel that is 5.92 mm at 1 MHz, so you need roughly 6 MHz or more.

tasks.label: Fire the same element into all three materials
tasks.hint: Same frequency, different velocity: lambda stretches in fast steel and shrinks in slow water.

ut1-4-2.title: Hands-on: peak the echo, put the flaw on the beam axis
ut1-4-2.intro: The spreading beam returns an echo over a whole range of probe positions, but amplitude is highest only when the hole sits on the beam axis. Practice this lesson's field rule, maximize before you evaluate, by sliding the wedge and marking each hidden hole exactly at its echo peak.

tasks.label: Sweep past hole 1 and mark dead on the echo peak
tasks.hint: The echo rises as the beam edge clips the hole; mark only when amplitude tops out.

tasks.label: Bracket the peak of echo 2, then mark it
tasks.hint: Slide past the maximum and come back; half amplitude means the hole is at the beam's 6 dB edge.

tasks.label: Put hole 3 on the beam axis and log it
tasks.hint: An echo that wanders as you move is beam geometry talking; mark where it peaks, not where it first appears.

ut1-4-3.title: Hands-on: the straight-beam workhorse on thin steps
ut1-4-3.intro: From the probe family tree, the 0-degree straight-beam contact probe doing its signature job. These steps are thin and out of order, so the short, well-damped pulse and the resolution you just read about are what separate one backwall echo from the next.

tasks.label: Gauge three different steps with the 0-degree probe
tasks.hint: Park the probe flat on a step and read the backwall echo; its time of flight tracks the metal under your wear face.

tasks.label: Pick out the thinnest step from its echo
tasks.hint: Thinner metal means a shorter round trip: the backwall echo that sits closest to the initial pulse.

ut1-4-4.title: Hands-on: thin vs thick, viscosity and wetting at work
ut1-4-4.intro: Borrowed from the penetrant bench: capillary wetting is the same physics that pulls couplant into every valley of a rough surface and floats the air out. Run a water-thin liquid, then a paste-thick one, and feel the trade-off behind the couplant menu: thin flows in fast but runs off, thick clings but crawls.

tasks.label: Run the thin grade and let it wick in completely
tasks.hint: Apply at the surface and wait: capillarity does the pulling, no pressure needed, just like couplant wetting into roughness valleys.

tasks.label: Re-run at maximum viscosity and compare fill times
tasks.hint: Slide viscosity to the paste end; the slow crawl you see is exactly what keeps heavy gel clinging to vertical and overhead work.

ut1-5-1.title: Hands-on: contact thickness survey on a step block
ut1-5-1.intro: Slide the 0° contact probe along the stepped block exactly as you would on a corrosion survey — only a thin couplant film separates the probe face from the steel. Flat, steady contact (not pressure) is what keeps the backwall echo repeatable while you gauge each step.

tasks.label: Record thickness readings on three different steps
tasks.hint: Keep the probe flat and still on each step — let the backwall echo settle before you record, just as a steady couplant film demands.

tasks.label: Identify the thinnest step on the block
tasks.hint: The thinnest step has the shortest round trip, so its backwall echo arrives earliest on the A-scan.

ut1-5-2.title: Hands-on: the 4:1 water-to-steel velocity rule
ut1-5-2.intro: Fire the same pulse through water and through steel and watch how much slower the couplant is — sound crosses water at roughly one quarter of its speed in steel. That 4:1 ratio is exactly why your immersion standoff must be at least thickness ÷ 4 (plus margin) before the water multiple clears the back-wall echo.

tasks.label: Send the pulse through water, then through steel
tasks.hint: Water carries sound at ~1,480 m/s vs steel's ~5,920 — that ~4× gap is the whole basis of the water-path ≥ thickness ÷ 4 rule.

tasks.label: Raise frequency until λ in steel is under 1 mm
tasks.hint: λ = v/f, so steel needs roughly 6 MHz or more — immersion's constant water coupling is what makes such high frequencies practical.

ut1-5-3.title: Hands-on: why air coupling runs below 1 MHz
ut1-5-3.intro: Drop the frequency to the slider's 1 MHz floor — already the ceiling of practical air-coupled work — and watch the wavelength stretch in water, aluminum, and steel. Velocity is the v in Z = ρv: air's ~330 m/s makes its impedance so low that R ≈ 1 at each interface, forcing air-coupled probes to low frequencies and coarser resolution.

tasks.label: Run the pulse through water, aluminum, and steel
tasks.hint: Compare velocities as you switch: water's 1,480 m/s already drags Z down, and air at ~330 m/s sits far lower still — hence the near-total reflection.

tasks.label: Push λ in steel below 1 mm — resolution air gives up
tasks.hint: You need about 6 MHz or more — far above the sub-1 MHz band that can cross an air gap, which is exactly the detail air coupling trades away.

ut1-6-1.title: Hands-on: locate 3 hidden holes with a 60° wedge
ut1-6-1.intro: Slide the 60° wedge along the scan line and Mark each side-drilled hole where its echo peaks — the same sound-path geometry you just plotted with skip and surface distance. The peak lands exactly where the refracted shear-beam centreline intersects the reflector.

tasks.label: Mark the first SDH at its echo peak
tasks.hint: Slide until the echo is tallest, then press Mark within ±4 mm — peak amplitude means the beam centreline is on the hole.

tasks.label: Sweep on and Mark the second hole's peak
tasks.hint: Watch the A-scan: the echo rises, peaks, and falls as the first-leg path sweeps through the hole — Mark at the top.

tasks.label: Pin the third hole from its sound path
tasks.hint: A deeper hole peaks with the wedge farther back — its surface distance is depth × tan 60°, just like your plot.

ut1-6-2.title: Hands-on: size the wavelength that rides the surface
ut1-6-2.intro: Rayleigh waves hug only the top wavelength of the part and Lamb modes need a plate just a few wavelengths thick, so λ = v/f decides where these waves live. Tune frequency and switch materials to see how big that ruler really is.

tasks.label: Push λ below 1 mm — a surface wave's whole world
tasks.hint: Raise the frequency: λ = v/f, so more MHz means a shorter wavelength — and a Rayleigh wave samples only about 1λ deep.

tasks.label: Run the pulse in steel, aluminium, and water
tasks.hint: Crest spacing tracks velocity at fixed frequency — and note water's short λ: fluids carry no shear or surface waves at all.

ut1-6-3.title: Hands-on: gauge thin steps where duals earn their keep
ut1-6-3.intro: Gauge a thin-stepped block and watch the back-wall echo crowd the initial pulse as the section shrinks. That squeeze is the ring-down dead zone that blinds a single element — exactly why dual-element TR probes own thin-section and near-surface work.

tasks.label: Gauge three steps of the thin-wall block
tasks.hint: Place the 0° probe squarely on a step and log the reading — on the 5 mm step the echo sits hard against the initial pulse.

tasks.label: Flag the thinnest step — dual-probe territory
tasks.hint: Thinnest means shortest time of flight: the back-wall echo closest to the initial pulse, where a dual's crossed beams focus.

ut1-6-4.title: Hands-on: feel the air gap a flat shoe leaves on pipe
ut1-6-4.intro: An eddy-current probe makes probe-to-part gap visible instantly: null it on full contact, then lift off and watch the signal run to AIR. That is the same coupling collapse a flat wedge suffers riding a curved surface on one contact line — the reason codes say contour once the gap tops about 0.5 mm.

tasks.label: Null the probe on full, mated contact
tasks.hint: Lower until height reads essentially zero, then press Null — like a contoured shoe, balance only counts on true contact.

tasks.label: Lift off 2 mm and watch the trace run to AIR
tasks.hint: Raise the probe slowly: every fraction of a millimetre of air gap drags the signal away — a rocking wedge does this on pipe.

ut1-7-1.title: Hands-on: raster a 0° beam like an immersion gantry
ut1-7-1.intro: An immersion gantry builds its picture from one primitive: a 0° beam held square to the entry surface while the backwall echo is logged position by position. Drive the probe across the stepped aluminum plate the way the bridge manipulator would, record three readings, and flag the thinnest step.

tasks.label: Log readings on three different steps
tasks.hint: Hold the probe steady mid-step until the backwall echo stops moving, then record.

tasks.label: Flag the thinnest step
tasks.hint: Thinner metal returns its backwall echo sooner — pick the step with the earliest echo.

ut1-7-2.title: Hands-on: see the 4:1 water-steel velocity ratio
ut1-7-2.intro: Minimum water path exists because sound crawls through water at roughly a quarter of its speed in steel — watch the same pulse in water, aluminum, and steel to see that 4:1 ratio yourself. Then raise the frequency until λ in steel drops below 1 mm, tightening the wavelength and near-field focus the way a focused immersion probe sharpens resolution.

tasks.label: Watch the pulse travel in all three materials
tasks.hint: Water runs ~1,480 m/s vs ~5,920 in steel — that 4:1 crawl is why a quarter-thickness water path suffices.

tasks.label: Shrink the wavelength in steel below 1 mm
tasks.hint: λ = v/f. Steel runs 5,920 m/s, so you need roughly 6 MHz or more.

ut1-7-3.title: Hands-on: locate hidden flaws with pulse-echo
ut1-7-3.intro: Through-transmission can only say the beam was blocked — pulse-echo tells you where. Using the same incident-angle control that generates plate waves, steer a 70° refracted beam through the plate and pin down all three hidden holes by their echo peaks: the locating power one-sided immersion scans rely on.

tasks.label: Mark the first hole at its echo peak
tasks.hint: Slide until the echo peaks, then press Mark within ±4 mm of the peak.

tasks.label: Sweep on and mark the second hole
tasks.hint: Each hole owns its own stretch of the scan line — watch for a fresh echo rising as you sweep.

tasks.label: Pin the third hole to finish the scan
tasks.hint: A deeper hole echoes later on the time base — don't confuse late with weak.

ut1-8-1.title: Hands-on: dial in the probe's near field
ut1-8-1.intro: The transducer is calibrated equipment: frequency and element diameter fix wavelength and the near field N = D^2 f / 4v. Tune the lesson's 10 mm, 5 MHz probe and watch the lambda and near-field readouts to see why amplitude references belong beyond N.

tasks.label: Push lambda below 1 mm in steel; watch N grow
tasks.hint: Raise frequency: lambda = v/f shrinks while N = D^2 f / 4v stretches the near field past the lesson's 21 mm example.

tasks.label: Fire the same pulse in steel, aluminum, water
tasks.hint: Hold frequency fixed and switch materials; velocity sets the crest spacing, so lambda and N shift with every material.

ut1-8-2.title: Hands-on: prove the time axis on known steps
ut1-8-2.intro: Standardization puts the screen's claims on trial against known references. Gauge thin and thick steps of a known block and confirm the sweep reads true: a constant offset would be a zero error, an error growing with distance a velocity error.

tasks.label: Record readings on three known steps
tasks.hint: Take one thin and one thick step among your three: zero error shifts every reading equally, velocity error scales with distance.

tasks.label: Call the thinnest step from the sweep alone
tasks.hint: The earliest backwall echo (shortest time of flight) marks the thinnest section, just as a calibrated sweep promises.

ut1-8-3.title: Hands-on: peak the SDH ladder, DAC-style
ut1-8-3.intro: Angle-beam standardization lives on side-drilled holes. Slide the 60-degree wedge along the reference plate and peak the echo from each SDH at increasing depth (about 1/4T, 1/2T, 3/4T) — the same peak-and-mark discipline used to set sensitivity and build a DAC.

tasks.label: Peak the first SDH echo and mark it
tasks.hint: Slide until the echo crests, then Mark within 4 mm of the peak — a DAC point is always taken at maximum response.

tasks.label: Capture the second SDH at maximum response
tasks.hint: Watch the echo rise and fall as the beam centreline sweeps the hole; mark the crest, not the first flicker.

tasks.label: Mark the third SDH to finish the ladder
tasks.hint: Deeper holes answer smaller — beam spread and attenuation at work, exactly why a DAC curve slopes downward.

ut1-8-4.title: Hands-on: same probe, different material
ut1-8-4.intro: Your reference gain was earned on one material, so carrying the probe elsewhere changes the transfer. Fire the same pulse through water (the couplant's bridging job made visible), steel, and aluminum to see why block-to-part differences demand transfer correction.

tasks.label: Run the pulse through all three materials
tasks.hint: Keep the settings fixed and switch materials — velocity and transmission change while your gain promise stays behind.

tasks.label: Drop lambda below 1 mm in steel
tasks.hint: Raise frequency in steel; shorter wavelengths scatter more in coarse grain, one reason a part never echoes like its block.

ut1-9-1.title: Hands-on: Straight-beam scan of the plate standard
ut1-9-1.intro: Procedure UT-SB-12 assigns a 0° straight-beam scan of a carbon-steel step standard spanning the procedure's 1–2.5 in. thickness scope. Gauge three steps to confirm a clean backwall echo across the whole scope, then call out the thinnest section.

tasks.label: Gauge three steps of the UT-SB-12 standard
tasks.hint: Drag the 0° probe onto a step; the backwall echo's time of flight reads its thickness.

tasks.label: Identify the thinnest step on the block
tasks.hint: Earliest backwall echo = least metal travel = the thinnest step.

ut1-9-2.title: Hands-on: Log readings, flag the reportable minimum
ut1-9-2.intro: A defensible straight-beam report starts with disciplined recording. These steps differ by only a few millimetres and sit in scrambled order — log each reading as you take it, then evaluate your own data and flag the minimum the report must carry.

tasks.label: Log three thickness readings as you go
tasks.hint: Record each reading the moment you take it — end-of-shift memory writes fiction, not data.

tasks.label: Flag the minimum reading for the report
tasks.hint: Steps are scrambled — trust your logged numbers, not the block's silhouette.

ut1-9-3.title: Hands-on: Mark the T/4, T/2, 3T/4 DAC holes
ut1-9-3.intro: Procedure UT-AB-7 puts a 60° shear wave on this 20 mm carbon-steel joint. Slide the wedge to peak and mark the side-drilled holes at T/4, T/2 and 3T/4 — the same reference reflectors the procedure's DAC is standardized on.

tasks.label: Peak and mark the T/4 hole (5 mm deep)
tasks.hint: Slide the wedge slowly and press Mark within ±4 mm of the echo's peak.

tasks.label: Peak and mark the T/2 hole (10 mm deep)
tasks.hint: Deeper hole, longer first-leg sound path — its echo peaks later in the sweep.

tasks.label: Peak and mark the 3T/4 hole (15 mm deep)
tasks.hint: The deepest hole needs the most stand-off: surface distance grows as depth × tan 60°.

ut1-9-4.title: Hands-on: Plot three reflectors from sound path
ut1-9-4.intro: Three reflectors hide at different depths under a 45° wedge in 25 mm plate. Peak and mark each echo, treating every mark as a plotted point — depth = s × cos 45°, surface distance = s × sin 45° on your report sketch.

tasks.label: Mark the 8 mm reflector at its echo peak
tasks.hint: Maximize the echo before you mark — off-peak position data plots fiction.

tasks.label: Mark the 12.5 mm reflector and plot depth
tasks.hint: All three sit in the first leg: sound path × cos 45° gives depth directly.

tasks.label: Mark the deep 19 mm reflector at its peak
tasks.hint: At 45° the surface distance equals the depth — both are s × 0.707.

ut2-1-1.title: Hands-on: dial in wavelength with λ = v/f
ut2-1-1.intro: Put λ = v/f under your fingers: sweep the probe frequency and jump between steel, aluminum, and water while the crest spacing and near-field readout respond live. This is the same velocity-and-wavelength reasoning this review expects you to run on every Level II problem.

tasks.label: Push λ in steel below 1 mm
tasks.hint: λ = v/f: with steel's 5920 m/s you need roughly 6 MHz or more.

tasks.label: Fire the pulse in steel, aluminum, and water
tasks.hint: Switch materials and watch crest spacing track velocity — slow water squeezes λ tightest.

ut2-1-2.title: Hands-on: read the A-scan on a stepped block
ut2-1-2.intro: Each probe position paints one A-scan — amplitude against time. Drag the 0° probe across the stepped block, read the backwall echo's time of flight as thickness, and notice how sweeping positions along a line is exactly how a B-scan gets stacked.

tasks.label: Record thickness readings on three different steps
tasks.hint: Doubling the thickness doubles the echo's time of flight — watch the backwall echo slide right.

tasks.label: Call the thinnest step from the A-scan alone
tasks.hint: Earliest backwall echo = shortest sound path.

ut2-1-3.title: Hands-on: angle-beam technique on a one-sided weld
ut2-1-3.intro: You have one accessible surface over a welded plate — a textbook case for the contact angle-beam technique. Slide the 70° wedge along the scan line and mark every hidden side-drilled hole, keeping coverage honest from first flaw to last.

tasks.label: Mark the first SDH echo as you scan the lane
tasks.hint: Slide steadily and press Mark within ±4 mm of where the echo peaks.

tasks.label: Keep scanning and mark the second hidden hole
tasks.hint: One hit is not coverage — the technique demands you sweep the full lane.

tasks.label: Sweep to the end and mark the third flaw
tasks.hint: The shear beam only answers when its centreline crosses a hole — mark at the peak, not the first rise.

ut2-1-4.title: Hands-on: prove the distance scale on known steps
ut2-1-4.intro: Standardization starts with the distance scale: prove it on references of known thickness before trusting anything the screen tells you. Read the 25, 50, 75, and 100 mm steps and confirm each backwall echo lands exactly where the scale says it should.

tasks.label: Verify readings on three known reference steps
tasks.hint: On a standardized scale the 25/50/75/100 mm references must read true and evenly spaced.

tasks.label: Identify the 25 mm reference step
tasks.hint: The thinnest reference returns the earliest backwall echo.

ut2-2-1.title: Hands-on: pick a wavelength the grain will tolerate
ut2-2-1.intro: As-cast ingot structure scatters sound roughly with the fourth power of frequency, so testers buy quiet screens by lengthening the wavelength. Sweep the probe frequency, watch λ = v/f respond in three materials, and feel why coarse-grained ingot work lives at the low end.

tasks.label: Push λ in steel below 1 mm — grain-noise territory
tasks.hint: λ = v/f: steel at 5,920 m/s needs roughly 6 MHz or more.

tasks.label: Send the pulse through steel, aluminium, and water
tasks.hint: Hold frequency steady — crest spacing tracks each material's velocity.

ut2-2-2.title: Hands-on: gauge the plate — spot the half-path echo
ut2-2-2.intro: A mid-wall lamination returns an echo at exactly half the backwall path — the same reading a half-thickness step gives. Gauge three steps of this rolled-plate block, then flag the thinnest, training your eye for the early echo that betrays a lamination.

tasks.label: Record straight-beam readings on three different steps
tasks.hint: Park the probe mid-step; the backwall echo's time of flight sets the depth.

tasks.label: Pick the thinnest step — the earliest backwall echo
tasks.hint: 12.5 mm reads exactly like 25 mm plate split by a mid-wall lamination.

ut2-2-3.title: Hands-on: hunt the centerline — mark both axial flaws
ut2-2-3.intro: Bar discontinuities are predictable: pipe remnants and stringers ride the axis at mid-section. Both hidden reflectors in this 30 mm section sit at centerline depth — slide the wedge, watch the echo rise, and press Mark at each peak.

tasks.label: Mark the first centerline reflector at its echo peak
tasks.hint: Slide slowly; press Mark within ±4 mm of where the echo tops out.

tasks.label: Find the second stringer riding the same mid-depth
tasks.hint: Same 15 mm depth, different spot along the scan — expect a twin echo farther on.

ut2-2-4.title: Hands-on: 45° shear scan — log OD, mid-wall, and ID flaws
ut2-2-4.intro: Tubular scanning launches a roughly 45° shear wave and judges everything against OD and ID notches. Sweep the wedge along this seam-scan analog and Mark three reflectors: a near-OD lap, a mid-wall hook crack, and a near-ID notch stand-in.

tasks.label: Mark the near-OD reflector — the lap analog
tasks.hint: Shallowest of the three: its echo arrives on the shortest beam path.

tasks.label: Catch the mid-wall hook-crack stand-in at its peak
tasks.hint: Mid-wall depth means a mid-length path — Mark within ±4 mm of the peak.

tasks.label: Log the near-ID reflector — your ID-notch analog
tasks.hint: Deepest reflector, longest path — like the half-V ID notch hit.

ut2-3-1.title: Hands-on: catch the flaws lying along the grain
ut2-3-1.intro: Forging flow lays stringers, flakes, and bursts parallel to the grain, so you fire a 45° shear beam across the flow to catch them. Slide the wedge along the forged section and Mark each hidden reflector right at its echo peak.

tasks.label: Mark the near-surface stringer 8 mm down
tasks.hint: Slide the wedge slowly and press Mark within ±4 mm of the echo peak.

tasks.label: Mark the centerline burst at 15 mm depth
tasks.hint: Peak amplitude means the 45° beam centreline is square on the flaw — bursts live at mid-wall.

tasks.label: Mark the deep flake cluster at 22 mm
tasks.hint: Deeper reflector, longer sound path — its echo arrives later on the A-scan.

ut2-3-2.title: Hands-on: shrink λ toward grain size — then back off
ut2-3-2.intro: Grain scatter climbs roughly with f⁴, which is why castings are tested slow. Raise the frequency until λ in steel drops below 1 mm — coarse-grain territory where grass buries the screen — then see how aluminum behaves at the same settings.

tasks.label: Push λ below 1 mm in steel — grain-scatter range
tasks.hint: λ = v/f: at 5,920 m/s you need roughly 6 MHz or more.

tasks.label: Send the same pulse through steel and aluminum
tasks.hint: Switch materials at a fixed frequency and watch λ and the near field shift.

ut2-3-3.title: Hands-on: read echo depth at composite velocity
ut2-3-3.intro: A delamination reflects nearly everything, so it reads like an early back wall at the separation depth. Gauge a stepped CFRP block at ~3,000 m/s — half steel's velocity — and pick out the thinnest step by its earliest echo.

tasks.label: Record readings on three laminate steps
tasks.hint: Center the 0° probe on a step and log the back-wall reading before moving on.

tasks.label: Flag the thinnest step — the earliest echo
tasks.hint: Thinner section, shorter time of flight: the echo sits closest to the initial pulse.

ut2-3-4.title: Hands-on: velocity sets the rules — steel to water
ut2-3-4.intro: Rubber carries sound at water speed — about a quarter of steel — and that one number resets the wavelength, the time base, and every readout. Fire the same pulse through water, aluminum, and steel and watch the picture change.

tasks.label: Run the pulse in water, aluminum, and steel
tasks.hint: Water stands in for rubber at ~1,480 m/s — watch the wavefronts bunch up versus steel.

tasks.label: In steel, drive λ under 1 mm; then recheck water
tasks.hint: Same frequency, a quarter the velocity: λ = v/f, so water's λ is ~4× shorter.

ut2-4-1.title: Hands-on: plan 60° angle-beam coverage of a butt weld
ut2-4-1.intro: Plan first-leg coverage of a 25 mm welded plate using the 60° wedge that strikes a 30° bevel square-on. Walk the wedge along the scan track and mark all three hidden side-drilled holes to prove your coverage plan reaches every depth.

tasks.label: Locate flaw 1 and Mark at the echo peak
tasks.hint: Slide slowly; press Mark within ±4 mm of where the echo peaks.

tasks.label: Locate flaw 2 — note its longer sound path
tasks.hint: Deeper holes echo later on the A-scan — depth = sound path × cos 60°.

tasks.label: Locate flaw 3 to complete full coverage
tasks.hint: Sweep the whole 240 mm track; coverage means every depth gets hit square-on.

ut2-4-2.title: Hands-on: orientation decides which cracks you see
ut2-4-2.intro: Weld flaws lie in predictable orientations — centerline, toe, transverse — and a field only reveals what it crosses near-perpendicular. Rotate the energized yoke over three differently oriented cracks and make each indicate, just as your scan plan must aim the beam square at each expected flaw.

tasks.label: Indicate the longitudinal (0°) centerline crack
tasks.hint: Rotate the yoke so flux crosses the crack near 90° — parallel flux leaks nothing.

tasks.label: Indicate the 45° toe crack
tasks.hint: Set the yoke axis near 135° so the pole-to-pole flux runs square across a 45° crack.

tasks.label: Indicate the transverse (90°) crack
tasks.hint: Flux must run along the weld axis (yoke near 0°) to cross a transverse crack.

ut2-4-3.title: Hands-on: maximize echoes and mark three weld flaws
ut2-4-3.intro: Interrogate each indication the way a Level II does: slide the 45° probe, watch the echo rise and fall, and mark only at the true peak. Three side-drilled holes at different depths drill the echo dynamics that separate real reflectors from geometry.

tasks.label: Peak the first echo, then press Mark
tasks.hint: Push past the peak and the echo collapses — back up to the crest before marking.

tasks.label: Characterize flaw 2: maximize, then mark
tasks.hint: Watch the rise–peak–fall as the 45° beam centreline sweeps across the hole.

tasks.label: Confirm flaw 3's peak before you call it
tasks.hint: Check its sound path — a real mid-wall reflector, not root or cap geometry.

ut2-4-4.title: Hands-on: pass or fail the setup against code limits
ut2-4-4.intro: Acceptance is the code's decision, not yours: a measured value either meets the written limit or it doesn't. Drive geometric unsharpness under its 0.5 mm spec and place the IQI exactly where the code demands — the same verify-against-the-book discipline weld UT dispositions require.

tasks.label: Drive Ug under the 0.5 mm acceptance limit
tasks.hint: Ug = f × OFD / SOD — pull the source back or close the film-side gap.

tasks.label: Place the wire IQI where the code requires
tasks.hint: Source-side weld surface only — film-side placement is rejected.

ut2-5-1.title: Hands-on: capillary flow — how adhesive fillets form
ut2-5-1.intro: Adhesive fillets climb honeycomb cell walls by the same capillary action that pulls penetrant into this magnified crack. Fill the crack, then race a thin grade against a thick one to see how viscosity decides whether a liquid wets a tight gap — or bridges it and leaves a void.

tasks.label: Apply penetrant and let capillarity fill the gap
tasks.hint: Apply and wait — fill depth grows as the square root of time (Washburn), the same wetting that builds core fillets during cure.

tasks.label: Fill at low and high viscosity; compare the curves
tasks.hint: Run one fill at each end of the viscosity slider — the depth-time chart overlays both runs, like runny paste adhesive versus stiff film.

ut2-5-2.title: Hands-on: read disbond depth from echo arrival
ut2-5-2.intro: A disbond turns its interface into an air-backed reflector, and pulse-echo's advantage is depth: echo arrival time tells you which bondline failed. Treat each step of this aluminum block as a deeper failed interface in a skin-doubler stack — gauge three, then flag the shallowest as the near-side disbond.

tasks.label: Gauge the reflector depth on three different steps
tasks.hint: Seat the 0-degree probe mid-step and read the backwall echo — an earlier arrival means sound stopped at a shallower interface.

tasks.label: Flag the shallowest step — the near-side disbond
tasks.hint: A near-side disbond stops sound at the first bondline, so its echo arrives earliest; a far-side break lets sound travel deeper first.

ut2-5-3.title: Hands-on: standardize on a reference — null, then deviate
ut2-5-3.intro: A bond evaluation is only valid after standardizing on a known-good reference: the tester is nulled there, and every finding is read as a deviation from that null. Practice the discipline — null the probe at contact, then lift off and watch the dot swing away exactly as it would over a disbonded, un-loaded skin.

tasks.label: Lower to contact and null on the known-good surface
tasks.hint: Get the probe within 0.15 mm of the surface before pressing Null — standardization demands real contact with the reference.

tasks.label: Lift off and watch the dot swing away from the null
tasks.hint: Raise the probe about 2 mm after a good null; a probe the structure no longer loads drifts toward AIR, like a meter over a disbond.

ut2-6-1.title: Hands-on: shrink the wavelength to catch small flaws
ut2-6-1.intro: Detection starts with the wave itself: sweep the probe frequency in a transparent slab and watch wavelength stretch and shrink as lambda = v/f. Make lambda short enough to catch a 1 mm reflector in steel, then see how aluminum and water rewrite it at the same frequency.

tasks.label: Raise frequency until lambda in steel drops below 1 mm
tasks.hint: Lambda = v/f. At 5920 m/s in steel you need roughly 6 MHz or more before a 1 mm reflector becomes a strong scatterer.

tasks.label: Pulse steel, aluminum, and water at one frequency
tasks.hint: Hold frequency fixed and switch materials: slower water (about 1480 m/s) packs the crests far closer together than steel.

ut2-6-2.title: Hands-on: resolve near-equal steps on a fine block
ut2-6-2.intro: These steps differ by as little as 2.5 mm, so their backwall echoes arrive only microseconds apart — separating and calling them is resolution plus operator discrimination. Gauge three steps, then commit to which one is thinnest.

tasks.label: Record thickness readings on three close steps
tasks.hint: Seat the 0-degree probe fully on one step at a time; the backwall echo's time of flight is your thickness reading.

tasks.label: Discriminate and pick the thinnest step
tasks.hint: Compare echo arrival times, not heights — the earliest backwall return marks the thinnest section.

ut2-6-3.title: Hands-on: peak each echo to size by probe motion
ut2-6-3.intro: Echo height alone is not size — what counts is how amplitude changes as the transducer moves. Sweep the 45-degree wedge across three side-drilled holes and mark each echo exactly at its peak, the same probe-motion discipline behind 6 dB-drop sizing.

tasks.label: Walk the beam over hole 1 and mark its echo peak
tasks.hint: Amplitude maximizes when the beam centreline hits the hole; mark within 4 mm of that peak.

tasks.label: Peak hole 2 as amplitude rises, crests, then falls
tasks.hint: Overshoot on purpose once — watching the echo drop past the peak is exactly what the 6 dB-drop method exploits.

tasks.label: Peak and mark hole 3 to finish the sizing scan
tasks.hint: Small probe steps near the crest separate the true peak from its shoulders.

ut2-6-4.title: Hands-on: locate three hidden holes with a 60-degree wedge
ut2-6-4.intro: Turn sweep time into position: each echo's soundpath plus the 60-degree wedge geometry fixes the reflector's depth and surface distance. Search the scan line systematically and mark all three hidden holes where their echoes peak.

tasks.label: Sweep the line and mark flaw 1 at maximum echo
tasks.hint: Move steadily until an echo appears, then jockey the wedge to peak it before pressing Mark.

tasks.label: Locate flaw 2 and read its depth from soundpath
tasks.hint: At 60 degrees, depth = soundpath x cos 60 — exactly half the soundpath on the first leg.

tasks.label: Locate flaw 3 to complete the position map
tasks.hint: Surface distance = soundpath x sin 60; a longer sweep time means a farther, deeper reflector on leg one.

ut2-7-1.title: Hands-on: verify a certified calibration standard
ut2-7-1.intro: An echo means nothing until it is compared with a calibrated standard. Verify this serialized block's certified step thicknesses with your own probe — same block, same setup, same answer — so every reading stays traceable to the certificate.

tasks.label: Verify certified thickness on three different steps
tasks.hint: Seat the 0° probe fully on a step and read the backwall echo — a certified 25 mm step must answer 25 mm on your screen too.

tasks.label: Confirm the standard: pick the thinnest certified step
tasks.hint: Compare backwall arrival times across steps, never echo heights — the earliest return marks the thinnest section.

ut2-7-2.title: Hands-on: peak the T/4, T/2, 3T/4 DAC reflector set
ut2-7-2.intro: A DAC curve is the same reference reflector peaked at increasing soundpaths. Slide the 60° wedge and peak the side-drilled holes at T/4, T/2, and 3T/4 of this 32 mm plate — the three points every distance-amplitude correction is drawn through.

tasks.label: Peak the T/4 reference hole — first DAC point
tasks.hint: Slide until amplitude crests, then press Mark within ±4 mm — the shortest soundpath anchors the top of the curve.

tasks.label: Peak the T/2 hole — the curve falls with distance
tasks.hint: Same reflector, longer soundpath: beam spread alone costs an SDH roughly 9 dB per doubling of distance.

tasks.label: Peak the 3T/4 hole to finish the DAC ladder
tasks.hint: Its echo sits latest on the sweep — and every future indication is judged against the curve at its own soundpath.

ut2-7-3.title: Hands-on: corrosion survey — find the minimum wall
ut2-7-3.intro: Two identical readings can earn opposite verdicts, because significance lives in the object. Service corrosion has thinned this nominal 25 mm wall unevenly: survey it and find the governing minimum — the number engineering weighs against the part's history and intended use.

tasks.label: Log remaining wall at three survey locations
tasks.hint: Drag the 0° probe from zone to zone — corrosion loss shows up as a backwall echo arriving earlier than nominal 25 mm.

tasks.label: Call the governing minimum wall for appraisal
tasks.hint: The thinnest reading rules the verdict — fitness depends on what duty the wall must still survive, not on the screen.

ut2-7-4.title: Hands-on: locate every indication for disposition
ut2-7-4.intro: No acceptance clause applies until the flaw has an address. Scan this 20 mm plate with the 60° wedge — the lesson's worked geometry — and mark every hidden indication at its echo peak so each can be located, classified, and judged to code.

tasks.label: Peak indication 1 and mark its address for the report
tasks.hint: Depth = SP·cos 60° and surface distance = SP·sin 60° — the lesson's worked triangle, now live on the sweep.

tasks.label: Locate indication 2 — no clause applies without it
tasks.hint: Acceptance tables key on type AND location: jockey the wedge to the amplitude crest, then Mark within ±4 mm.

tasks.label: Complete the record: mark the last indication
tasks.hint: A defensible report lists every recordable indication with datum, depth, and surface distance — leave none unlogged.
